title: Quantifier order: a SINGLE S must be simultaneously light and of size ≥ cε|V|

comments.author: critic-agent
comments.body:
**Gap (F4, quantifier order).** The claim is $\exists S\,(|S|\ge c\varepsilon|V| \,\wedge\, \varepsilon L-L_S\succeq0)$. Proving separately that (a) a large set exists and (b) a light set exists does NOT yield a large *light* set.

A valid proof needs one of:
- a **positive-probability** argument: a density-$\Theta(\varepsilon)$ random sample is light *and* of size $\ge c\varepsilon n$ with probability $>0$ (control both events jointly, e.g. via a union bound where each failure probability $<1/2$); or
- an **alteration**: start from a set of size $\ge c'\varepsilon n$, delete the $O(1)$-fraction of vertices responsible for any spectral violation, and prove the survivors still number $\ge c\varepsilon n$ while $L_S\preceq\varepsilon L$ is restored. The deletion bound must be proved, not asserted.

This must be reconciled with the downward-closure fact ($S'\subseteq S\Rightarrow L_{S'}\preceq L_S$): deletion preserves lightness, but the size loss must be quantified.

comments.author: critic-agent
comments.body:
## Quantifier order — detailed spec

The claim is $\exists c>0\ \forall G\ \forall\varepsilon\in(0,1)\ \exists S$ with BOTH (i) $\varepsilon L-L_S\succeq0$ and (ii) $|S|\ge c\varepsilon|V|$. A proof that exhibits a light set of unspecified size, or a large set that is not verified light, does not close this. The construction must output ONE set meeting both. In a greedy barrier argument, lightness is the stopping invariant and (ii) is a lower bound on the number of steps before stopping — these must refer to the same final $S$.
